easyHint: Sleeping on the job is highly encouraged to steal secrets.
mediumHint: The architect's name is a Greek mythological reference to a maze maker.
hardHint: Edith Piaf's song was chosen because its length matches the runtime proportionally when slowed down.

easyHint: A farmer leaves his cornfields to find a new home in the stars.
mediumHint: The ticking sound on the water planet represents one day passing on Earth for every tick.
hardHint: The dust storms were real, created using massive fans and cellulose-based synthetic dust.

easyHint: Take the red pill and wake up.
mediumHint: The falling green code is actually scanned sushi recipes from a Japanese cookbook.
hardHint: Will Smith famously turned down the lead role to star in Wild Wild West.

easyHint: The first rule is we don't talk about it.
mediumHint: There is a Starbucks coffee cup hidden in almost every frame of the movie.
hardHint: The author of the original book got the idea after getting beaten up while camping and no one at work asked him about his bruises.

easyHint: A glowing briefcase and a Royale with Cheese.
mediumHint: The clocks in the pawn shop are all set to 4:20.
hardHint: The plaster on the mob boss's neck is never explained but was just to cover an actual shaving cut the actor had.

easyHint: An offer you can't refuse.
mediumHint: The presence of oranges always foreshadows a death or a close call.
hardHint: The word 'mafia' is never spoken in the film due to an agreement with the actual mob.

easyHint: A poor family slowly infiltrates a wealthy household, one job at a time.
mediumHint: The rock given to the son is a 'suseok' or scholar's rock, meant to bring wealth.
hardHint: The rich house wasn't real; it was a set built from scratch on an outdoor lot, designed specifically for the camera's aspect ratio.

easyHint: Life is like a box of chocolates.
mediumHint: The main character's eyes are closed in every single photograph shown in the movie.
hardHint: The actor running across America was often actually the lead star's brother, Jim, acting as a double.

easyHint: A wrongly convicted banker finds hope and a poster of Rita Hayworth.
mediumHint: The mugshot of the young Red is actually a picture of Morgan Freeman's son.
hardHint: The Stephen King novella it's based on was part of a collection called 'Different Seasons', which also gave us 'Stand By Me'.

easyHint: Why so serious?
mediumHint: The hospital explosion was unscripted in its pause; the actor really improvised fidgeting with the detonator.
hardHint: The mayor in the movie is played by an actor who was also the original voice of the animated series' villain, Two-Face.

easyHint: Half the universe was snapped away, now they want them back.
mediumHint: The rat that steps on the quantum tunnel controls is the true savior of the universe.
hardHint: The sound heard after the credits is the sound of Tony Stark hammering his first suit in a cave.

easyHint: A doomed ship, an iceberg, and a door that clearly had enough space for two.
mediumHint: The sketch of the female lead was actually drawn by the film's director, not the lead actor.
hardHint: The older version of the female lead owned a Pomeranian, which was one of the three actual dogs known to survive the real sinking.

easyHint: Blue aliens on Pandora protect their magical tree.
mediumHint: The language spoken by the aliens was created by a linguistics professor and has over 1,000 words.
hardHint: The cigarette smoked by the lead scientist is entirely CGI; the actress refused to smoke on set.

easyHint: A theme park where the exhibits eat the tourists.
mediumHint: The T-Rex roar is a combination of a baby elephant, a tiger, and an alligator.
hardHint: The water rippling in the cup was achieved by plucking a guitar string attached beneath the dashboard.

word: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
easyHint: Destroying a piece of jewelry in a volcano to save Middle-earth.
mediumHint: The actor playing the main villain broke his toe kicking an Uruk-hai helmet in a previous film.
hardHint: The entire ending sequence was shot multiple times over weeks because the actors couldn't stop crying during the goodbye scene.

easyHint: Are you not entertained? A betrayed general fights in the Colosseum.
mediumHint: The wounds on the lead actor's face after the opening battle were real, caused by his horse backing him into branches.
hardHint: The thumbs down gesture historically meant 'swords down' (spare him), not kill, but the director kept it as the modern audience understands it.

easyHint: A cannibal helps an FBI trainee catch a killer with an affinity for moths.
mediumHint: The lead villain never blinks while speaking to the FBI agent.
hardHint: The movie is the third in history to win the 'Big Five' Academy Awards, but the lead actor was only on screen for 16 minutes.

easyHint: A businessman saves his Jewish workers during the Holocaust.
mediumHint: The girl in the red coat is the only element of color in the main black-and-white portion of the film.
hardHint: The director refused to accept a salary for the film, calling it 'blood money'.

easyHint: A squad risks everything to bring one mother's last surviving son home.
mediumHint: All the actors went through a grueling boot camp, except the actor they were rescuing, so the squad would actually resent him.
hardHint: The D-Day landing scene was so realistic that the VA set up a special 800 number for veterans who experienced PTSD after watching it.

easyHint: A death row corrections officer meets an inmate with miraculous healing powers.
mediumHint: The mouse, Mr. Jingles, was played by 15 different mice.
hardHint: The lead actor had a custom-built slightly smaller bed to make the massive inmate look even larger in comparison.

easyHint: What's in the box?! A serial killer uses deadly sins as his motif.
mediumHint: All the killer's notebooks shown were fully handwritten and took two months to create.
hardHint: The studio tried to change the dark ending, but the lead actor refused to do the movie unless the original ending was kept.

easyHint: A cop undercover in the mob, and a mobster undercover in the cops in Boston.
mediumHint: An 'X' motif appears hidden in the background every time a character is about to die.
hardHint: This film is actually an English-language remake of the 2002 Hong Kong film 'Infernal Affairs'.

easyHint: Two rival magicians in Victorian London go to extreme lengths to top each other.
mediumHint: The initials of the main characters, Alfred and Robert, spell A.B.R.A.
hardHint: David Bowie plays Nikola Tesla, and his scenes were shot entirely in one week to accommodate his touring schedule.

easyHint: A freed slave partners with a bounty hunter to rescue his wife from a brutal plantation owner.
mediumHint: The actor playing the villain really smashed a glass and cut his hand badly during a scene but kept acting through the take.
hardHint: The blue suit worn by the lead character was inspired by the famous painting 'The Blue Boy' by Thomas Gainsborough.

easyHint: A post-apocalyptic road trip involving a guy attached to the front of a car playing a flame-throwing guitar.
mediumHint: Over 80% of the effects seen in the film are real practical effects, not CGI.
hardHint: The movie was heavily delayed for years because unexpected heavy rains caused the Australian desert shooting location to bloom with flowers.

easyHint: A chemistry teacher turns to cooking meth in an RV.
mediumHint: The pizza on the roof was thrown perfectly in a single take.
hardHint: The coordinates of the buried money actually point to the Albuquerque studios where the show was filmed.

easyHint: Winter is coming, and everyone is fighting for an iron chair.
mediumHint: The Night's Watch cloaks are actually modified Ikea rugs.
hardHint: A coffee cup famously appeared in the final season, but earlier, a Starbucks cup was also spotted in season 4.

easyHint: Kids in the 80s fight monsters from the Upside Down.
mediumHint: The kids' D&D campaign foreshadows the monster they face in the season.
hardHint: The sensory deprivation tank required 1,200 pounds of Epsom salt to make the actress float.

easyHint: A mockumentary about the daily lives of paper company employees in Scranton.
mediumHint: The computers on the set actually had internet access, so the actors were genuinely browsing the web during scenes.
hardHint: John Krasinski shot the footage of Scranton shown in the opening credits from his own car.

easyHint: Six pals hang out at Central Perk coffee shop.
mediumHint: The golden frame on the door was originally a mirror that a crew member accidentally broke.
hardHint: Bruce Willis appeared on the show for free because he lost a bet to Matthew Perry over their movie 'The Whole Nine Yards'.

easyHint: A New Jersey mob boss balances family life with 'this thing of ours' and sees a therapist.
mediumHint: The Bada Bing strip club was a real active strip club in New Jersey called Satin Dolls.
hardHint: The opening credits show the Twin Towers in the rearview mirror for the first three seasons, but they were removed after 9/11.

easyHint: A gritty look at the drug trade in Baltimore, showing both sides of the law.
mediumHint: Many of the minor characters were played by real former Baltimore criminals, politicians, and police officers.
hardHint: The iconic quote 'all the pieces matter' is a direct nod to the show's intense focus on interconnected municipal institutions.

easyHint: A Birmingham gang sews razor blades into their caps in the aftermath of WWI.
mediumHint: The lead actor smoked over 3,000 herbal cigarettes per season.
hardHint: Despite the show's name, historical records suggest the real gang rarely used razor blades in their hats due to the high cost of the blades at the time.

easyHint: A German sci-fi thriller about time travel, missing children, and complex family trees.
mediumHint: The phrase 'Sic Mundus Creatus Est' is central to the mystery.
hardHint: The show perfectly predicted the date of an apocalypse in 2020, airing years before the global pandemic.

easyHint: An anthology series showing the dark, dystopian side of modern technology.
mediumHint: The song 'Anyone Who Knows What Love Is' appears in almost every season.
hardHint: The title of the show refers to the cold, reflective screen of a turned-off television or smartphone.

easyHint: A dramatization of the reign of Queen Elizabeth II.
mediumHint: The actors playing the Queen change every two seasons to reflect her aging.
hardHint: The Queen's beloved corgis on set were reportedly very poorly behaved and often ruined takes.

easyHint: A wealthy media family backstabs each other to take over the aging patriarch's company.
mediumHint: The theme song uses a slightly out-of-tune piano to sound both grand and slightly 'off'.
hardHint: The show is heavily inspired by the Murdoch family, but the creator refuses to officially confirm it.

easyHint: A lone bounty hunter travels the galaxy protecting a powerful green child.
mediumHint: The lead actor is rarely actually inside the armor; it's often stunt doubles.
hardHint: The revolutionary 'Volume' LED stage technology was first heavily used and popularized by this production.

easyHint: A prequel showing how a struggling lawyer became a criminal's best friend.
mediumHint: The intro sequence degrades in quality every season, symbolizing the character's moral decay.
hardHint: The Cinnabon scenes in Omaha were actually filmed in a mall in Albuquerque.

easyHint: The rise and fall of Pablo Escobar and the DEA agents hunting him.
mediumHint: The lead actor playing the Colombian drug lord is actually Brazilian and had to learn Spanish for the role.
hardHint: The show uses real archival footage interspersed with the drama to show the sheer absurdity of the true events.

easyHint: A group of vigilantes take down corrupt, corporate-owned superheroes.
mediumHint: The actor playing the main villain based his terrifying smile on a specific political figure.
hardHint: In the comics, the main protagonist is meant to look exactly like Simon Pegg, who ends up playing the character's father in the show.

easyHint: Desperate people play deadly children's games for a massive cash prize in Korea.
mediumHint: The creepy giant doll from 'Red Light, Green Light' was a real statue temporarily borrowed from a museum.
hardHint: The phone number shown on the mysterious calling card belonged to a real person who received thousands of calls a day until Netflix edited it out.

easyHint: A dramatized account of the 1986 nuclear disaster in the Soviet Union.
mediumHint: The creator recorded actual ambient sound inside a decommissioned nuclear plant in Lithuania for the show's unnerving score.
hardHint: The character of Ulana Khomyuk did not exist in real life; she was created as a composite character to represent the many scientists who helped investigate the disaster.

easyHint: An anthology crime drama famous for a dark, philosophical first season in Louisiana.
mediumHint: The six-minute long tracking shot in a raid sequence was done entirely without hidden cuts.
hardHint: The show's creator was heavily accused of plagiarizing the bleak, nihilistic dialogue of the main character from the works of Thomas Ligotti.

easyHint: A futuristic theme park where wealthy guests live out fantasies with lifelike androids.
mediumHint: The player piano in the saloon frequently plays modern songs, like Radiohead covers.
hardHint: The show is based on a 1973 movie directed by Michael Crichton, who later wrote Jurassic Park.

easyHint: A socially anxious hacker gets involved in taking down the largest corporate conglomerate in the world.
mediumHint: All the hacking seen on screen is highly accurate and uses real, functioning Linux commands and tools.
hardHint: The entire series' twist ending was subtly hinted at in a single throwaway line in the pilot episode regarding an imaginary friend.

easyHint: Two FBI agents in the 1970s start interviewing imprisoned serial killers to understand their psychology.
mediumHint: The dialogue spoken by the serial killers is often taken word-for-word from real transcripts.
hardHint: The director used CGI to slightly alter the landscapes, removing modern street signs and adding period-accurate trees that were barely noticeable.

easyHint: An anthology black comedy crime series set in the snowy Midwest.
mediumHint: Every episode falsely claims 'This is a true story' at the beginning.
hardHint: The second season heavily features a UFO motif, ending in a literal flying saucer appearance that genuinely baffled some viewers.

easyHint: Employees undergo a procedure to separate their work memories from their personal memories.
mediumHint: The waffle party is the highest honor an employee can earn.
hardHint: The set design uses perfectly symmetrical corridors and specifically chosen low ceilings to induce a mild feeling of claustrophobia in the viewer.

easyHint: A smuggler guides a teenage girl across a post-apocalyptic America devastated by a fungal infection.
mediumHint: The terrifying clicking sound made by the infected was performed by the same voice actors who did it for the video game.
hardHint: The show's creators deliberately avoided having the actors play the video game so their performances wouldn't just be imitations.

easyHint: The staple Indian glucose biscuit featuring a young girl on the wrapper.
mediumHint: During the 2020 lockdown, it recorded its highest sales in its 82-year history.
hardHint: The 'G' originally stood for 'Gluco', but was later rebranded to mean 'Genius'.

easyHint: The taste of India, famous for its butter and iconic cartoon girl in a polka dot dress.
mediumHint: It is a cooperative brand managed by the Gujarat Co-operative Milk Marketing Federation.
hardHint: The brand's name is derived from the Sanskrit word 'Amulya', meaning priceless.

easyHint: A massive Indian conglomerate that makes everything from salt to software and cars.
mediumHint: They acquired Jaguar Land Rover from Ford in 2008.
hardHint: The founder built the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel in Mumbai because he was allegedly refused entry to a 'whites only' hotel.

easyHint: The conglomerate behind India's telecom revolution with 'Jio'.
mediumHint: Its founder started his career as a gas station attendant in Yemen.
hardHint: The company's logo color palette heavily features a specific shade of blue, known internally as 'Dhirubhai Blue'.

easyHint: The classic thumping motorcycle brand beloved in India, known for the 'Bullet'.
mediumHint: It's the oldest global motorcycle brand in continuous production.
hardHint: The original company also made lawnmowers and bicycles in Redditch, England, before moving solely to India.

easyHint: The 2-minute noodles that are a staple late-night snack in India.
mediumHint: It was banned in India in 2015 due to excessive lead, causing a massive nationwide uproar.
hardHint: The brand actually originated in Switzerland in the 19th century as a way to improve the nutritional intake of working families.

easyHint: India's homegrown e-commerce giant, started by two guys named Sachin and Binny.
mediumHint: They started by exclusively selling books before expanding into everything else.
hardHint: Walmart acquired a massive 77% controlling stake in this company in 2018 for $16 billion.

easyHint: A prominent Indian restaurant aggregator and food delivery startup in red.
mediumHint: It was originally called 'Foodiebay' before being rebranded.
hardHint: They once acquired a company called 'Urbanspoon' solely to enter the US and Australian markets.

easyHint: The major orange-colored food delivery competitor in India.
mediumHint: They launched 'Instamart' for 15-minute grocery deliveries.
hardHint: The company was born out of a logistics startup called 'Bundl' which the founders had initially started to fix courier issues.

easyHint: The digital wallet that became ubiquitous in India after demonetization in 2016.
mediumHint: Its name is an acronym for 'Pay Through Mobile'.
hardHint: Its IPO in 2021 was the largest in Indian history at the time, but the stock subsequently crashed on listing day.

easyHint: Indian manufacturer known for rugged SUVs like the Scorpio and Thar.
mediumHint: They are the largest manufacturer of tractors in the world by volume.
hardHint: The company originally assembled Willys Jeeps in India under license just after independence.

easyHint: The company that motorized India with the 'Chetak' scooter.
mediumHint: Their tagline 'Hamara Bajaj' is one of the most recognizable ad jingles in India.
hardHint: They stopped manufacturing scooters entirely in 2009 to focus on motorcycles before reviving the Chetak as an EV.

easyHint: The telecom network that brought ultra-cheap 4G data to India.
mediumHint: It reached 50 million subscribers in just 83 days after launch.
hardHint: The logo is a mirror image of the word 'OIL', nodding to the parent company's origins.

easyHint: Indian food company famous for Good Day and Tiger biscuits.
mediumHint: Their classic jingle has a distinct 'Ting Ting Ti-Ting' bell sound.
hardHint: During WWII, they supplied biscuits strictly to the British Indian Army, halting commercial sales.

easyHint: The ultimate brand for Indian bhujia, namkeen, and traditional sweets.
mediumHint: It started as a small retail sweets and namkeen shop in Bikaner, Rajasthan.
hardHint: Despite its traditional image, its revenue consistently dwarfs the Indian operations of major western junk food brands like McDonald's.

easyHint: The legendary dark rum that is a cult favorite in India.
mediumHint: It has arguably never officially advertised yet retains massive market share.
hardHint: It is produced by Mohan Meakin, a brewery founded by a British entrepreneur in Kasauli in the 1850s.

easyHint: The ubiquitous amber-colored antiseptic liquid in Indian households.
mediumHint: The distinctive smell comes from its active ingredient, chloroxylenol.
hardHint: It is not an Indian brand; it's owned by the British company Reckitt, though India is its biggest market.

easyHint: The bright red, strong-smelling soap historically marketed for health and hygiene.
mediumHint: It was originally introduced in 1895 as a carbolic soap.
hardHint: It is technically not classified as 'soap' in some countries anymore due to regulatory definitions, but rather as a 'syndet' bar.

easyHint: The Indo-Japanese automaker that put middle-class India on four wheels with the '800'.
mediumHint: They hold nearly 50% of the entire Indian passenger car market.
hardHint: The company was originally founded by Sanjay Gandhi, but he died before the first car rolled out, leading to the Japanese partnership.

easyHint: The world's largest manufacturer of two-wheelers, originally partnered with Honda.
mediumHint: Their 'Splendor' model is the highest-selling motorcycle in the world.
hardHint: The founders started by selling bicycle spare parts in Amritsar before the partition of India.

easyHint: The leading paint company in India with a mascot named 'Gattu'.
mediumHint: The mascot was created by the famous Indian cartoonist R.K. Laxman.
hardHint: They owe their massive market dominance to an early supercomputer they bought in 1970 to forecast color demand across India.

easyHint: The strong, fizzy cola brand in India that 'tastes the thunder'.
mediumHint: It was bought by Coca-Cola in the 90s just to kill it, but its popularity forced them to keep it alive.
hardHint: It was created in the 70s as a direct replacement when Coca-Cola was kicked out of India by the government.

easyHint: The Indian brand synonymous with steel almirahs and locks.
mediumHint: They made the first ballot boxes for independent India's first election in 1951.
hardHint: The founder started the company making locks because he read about a rising burglary rate in Bombay in a newspaper.

easyHint: A major Indian two-wheeler manufacturer based in Chennai, known for the Apache.
mediumHint: They are the only two-wheeler manufacturer to have won the Deming Prize for total quality management.
hardHint: The company actually started by running a rural bus service in Madurai in 1911.

easyHint: The massive commercial truck and bus manufacturer, the backbone of Indian transport.
mediumHint: They are the second-largest manufacturer of commercial vehicles in India after Tata.
hardHint: The company was named after the founder's son (Ashok) and the British company they partnered with (Leyland).

easyHint: A decentralized digital ledger used to record transactions securely.
mediumHint: It prevents the 'double-spending' problem in digital currency.
hardHint: The concept was first theorized in 1991 by Stuart Haber and W. Scott Stornetta for cryptographically securing documents.

easyHint: Machines programmed to mimic human cognitive functions.
mediumHint: The Turing Test is a classic measure of this technology's success.
hardHint: The term was coined at the Dartmouth Conference in 1956 by John McCarthy.

easyHint: Computers that use qubits, which can be both 0 and 1 at the same time.
mediumHint: They operate at temperatures close to absolute zero.
hardHint: Shor's algorithm is feared because it could theoretically break modern RSA encryption using this tech.

easyHint: A simulated 3D environment you explore using a headset.
mediumHint: Oculus Rift popularized its modern revival before being bought by Meta.
hardHint: The 'Sensorama' from 1962 is considered one of the earliest functioning prototypes of this technology.

easyHint: Overlaying digital information onto the real world through a screen or glasses.
mediumHint: Pokémon GO is the most famous mainstream example.
hardHint: The heads-up display (HUD) in military fighter jets was one of the first practical applications.

easyHint: Everyday physical devices connected to the web, like smart fridges and bulbs.
mediumHint: The Mirai botnet maliciously used these devices to take down major websites.
hardHint: A toaster connected to the internet by John Romkey in 1990 is widely considered the first device of this kind.

easyHint: The fifth generation of mobile network technology offering faster speeds.
mediumHint: It utilizes higher-frequency millimeter waves, which require more localized towers.
hardHint: During its rollout, bizarre conspiracy theories falsely linked it to the spread of COVID-19.

easyHint: Storing and accessing data and programs over the internet instead of your computer's hard drive.
mediumHint: AWS, Azure, and Google are the 'Big Three' providers.
hardHint: The term was first used in an internal Compaq business plan in 1996.

easyHint: A subset of AI where systems learn from data rather than explicit programming.
mediumHint: Neural networks are a key architecture modeled after the human brain.
hardHint: Arthur Samuel coined the term in 1959 while working at IBM on computer checkers.

easyHint: Digital or virtual money secured by cryptography.
mediumHint: Bitcoin is the first and most famous example.
hardHint: David Chaum's 'eCash' in the 1980s was an early predecessor to this concept.

easyHint: A unique digital identifier used to certify authenticity and ownership of digital art.
mediumHint: Bored Apes and CryptoPunks are famous collections.
hardHint: The first one ever created was 'Quantum' by Kevin McCoy in 2014, long before the hype.

easyHint: A hypothesized iteration of the internet as a single, universal virtual world.
mediumHint: Mark Zuckerberg renamed his entire company to focus on this.
hardHint: The term was coined by Neal Stephenson in his 1992 science fiction novel 'Snow Crash'.

easyHint: Unmanned aerial vehicles controlled remotely.
mediumHint: They are increasingly used for delivery, videography, and agriculture.
hardHint: The earliest recorded use of an unmanned vehicle for bombing was Austria attacking Venice in 1849 with balloon carriers.

easyHint: Making a physical object from a three-dimensional digital model by laying down layers of material.
mediumHint: Also known as additive manufacturing.
hardHint: Charles Hull invented stereolithography, the foundation of this tech, in 1983.

easyHint: Cars that drive themselves without human intervention.
mediumHint: Waymo and Cruise are major companies developing this.
hardHint: The DARPA Grand Challenge in 2004 kickstarted the modern push, though no car finished the course that year.

easyHint: Protecting systems, networks, and programs from digital attacks.
mediumHint: Phishing and ransomware are common threats to this field.
hardHint: The first computer worm, the Morris Worm (1988), accidentally highlighted the need for this entire industry.

easyHint: Extremely large sets of information analyzed computationally to reveal patterns.
mediumHint: It is characterized by the 3 Vs: Volume, Velocity, and Variety.
hardHint: The concept took off in the early 2000s when Doug Laney articulated the 3 Vs framework.

easyHint: Processing data closer to where it's being generated rather than in a centralized data-processing warehouse.
mediumHint: Crucial for self-driving cars so they don't have to wait for server ping times.
hardHint: Akamai's content delivery networks in the late 90s are often cited as the precursor to this paradigm.

easyHint: Using physical characteristics like fingerprints or faces to identify individuals.
mediumHint: Apple's FaceID is a widespread consumer application.
hardHint: The Alphonse Bertillon system of body measurements in the 19th century was the first scientific system of this kind.

easyHint: The study of an organism's complete set of DNA.
mediumHint: CRISPR is a tool used to edit this code.
hardHint: The Human Genome Project took 13 years and cost roughly $3 billion, while today it takes hours and under $1000.

easyHint: Manipulating matter on an atomic or molecular scale.
mediumHint: Used in medicine to deliver drugs precisely to cells.
hardHint: Richard Feynman's 1959 talk 'There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom' is considered the conceptual origin.

easyHint: The intersection of science, engineering, and tech that produces machines that substitute for human actions.
mediumHint: Boston Dynamics is famous for making viral videos of these machines doing parkour.
hardHint: The word itself was coined by science fiction author Isaac Asimov in 1941.

easyHint: Smart electronic devices worn close to or on the surface of the skin.
mediumHint: Smartwatches and fitness trackers are the most common examples.
hardHint: A 17th-century abacus ring from Qing Dynasty China is often jokingly referred to as the first.

easyHint: A residence that uses internet-connected devices to enable remote monitoring and management of appliances.
mediumHint: Alexa, turn off the lights.
hardHint: ECHO IV, created in 1966, was an early prototype that could control room temperature and turn appliances on/off.

easyHint: Power that comes from renewable, zero-emission sources.
mediumHint: Solar and wind are the most prominent examples.
hardHint: The first known use of a windmill to generate electricity was in Scotland in 1887.
